Sentiment Analysis of Game of Thrones by Clara Laursen & Liva Rask.

updated 27 March, 2026

Research question

What does the distribution of AFINN sentiment scores suggest about the emotional tone of Game of Thrones?

Method

The text was extracted from the PDF, tokenized into words, cleaned by removing stop words and numbers, and analysed with the AFINN and NRC sentiment lexicons.

Data

got_path <- here("data","got.pdf")
got_text <- pdf_text(got_path)

Cleaning and structuring the text

The extracted PDF text was split into lines, unnested into rows, and trimmed to remove extra whitespace before tokenization.

got_df <- data.frame(got_text) %>%
  mutate(text_full = str_split(got_text, pattern = '\n')) %>%
  unnest(text_full) %>%
  mutate(text_full = str_trim(text_full))

Each line from the PDF was stored as a separate row.

Preparing words for analysis

The text was tokenized into individual words using unnest_tokens().

got_tokens <- got_df %>%
  unnest_tokens(word, text_full)

Counting the words:

got_wc <- got_tokens %>%
  count(word) %>%
  arrange(-n)

Removing stop words:

Common stop words were removed using anti_join(stop_words). The remaining words were then counted again to identify the most frequent meaningful terms in the text.

tidyr::anti_join():

got_stop <- got_tokens %>%
  anti_join(stop_words)
got_swc <- got_stop %>%
  count(word) %>%
  arrange(-n)

Removing numeric tokens

got_no_numeric <- got_stop %>%
  filter(is.na(as.numeric(word)))

Numeric tokens were removed so that the analysis focused on words rather than page numbers or other non-text elements.

Preparing data for the word cloud

This word cloud visualises the most frequent meaningful words in Game of Thrones.

length(unique(got_no_numeric$word))
## [1] 11209
got_top100 <- got_no_numeric %>%
  count(word) %>%
  arrange(-n) %>%
  head(100)

got_top100
## # A tibble: 100 × 2
##    word       n
##    <chr>  <int>
##  1 lord    1341
##  2 ser     1023
##  3 jon      787
##  4 ned      743
##  5 tyrion   591
##  6 eyes     567
##  7 hand     567
##  8 king     542
##  9 father   512
## 10 told     504
## # ℹ 90 more rows
got_cloud <- ggplot(data = got_top100, aes(label = word)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud() +
  theme_minimal()

got_cloud

This version highlights the most frequent words more clearly by scaling word size and colour according to frequency:

ggplot(data = got_top100, aes(label = word, size = n)) +
  geom_text_wordcloud_area(aes(color = n), shape = "star") +
  scale_size_area(max_size = 12) +
  scale_color_gradientn(colors = c("darkgreen", "blue", "red")) +
  theme_minimal()

## Sentiment analysis To analyse the emotional vocabulary of Game of Thrones, I used the AFINN and NRC lexicons. AFINN assigns words scores from negative to positive, while NRC groups words into emotion categories such as fear, trust, joy, and sadness.

got_afinn <- got_stop %>%
  inner_join(get_sentiments("afinn"))
got_afinn
## # A tibble: 13,058 × 3
##    got_text                                                          word  value
##    <chr>                                                             <chr> <dbl>
##  1 "                  A GAME OF THRONES\n\n\n            Book One o… fire     -2
##  2 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
##  3 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
##  4 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… smile     2
##  5 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
##  6 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
##  7 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
##  8 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
##  9 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… dead     -3
## 10 "                              previous | Table of Contents | ne… drag     -1
## # ℹ 13,048 more rows

The matched words were counted by AFINN score to show the overall distribution of negative and positive terms in the text.

got_afinn_hist <- got_afinn %>% 
  count(value)

ggplot(data = got_afinn_hist, aes(x = value, y = n)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = value)) +
  theme_bw()

Investigate some of the words in a bit more depth:

got_afinn2 <- got_afinn %>% 
  filter(value == 2)
unique(got_afinn2$word)
##   [1] "smile"           "fine"            "glory"           "hope"           
##   [5] "smiled"          "care"            "strength"        "peaceful"       
##   [9] "honor"           "carefully"       "slick"           "top"            
##  [13] "gained"          "comfort"         "sweet"           "courage"        
##  [17] "daring"          "elegant"         "justice"         "heroes"         
##  [21] "fair"            "strong"          "brave"           "solid"          
##  [25] "proud"           "mercy"           "rescue"          "swift"          
##  [29] "smiling"         "true"            "noble"           "saved"          
##  [33] "gift"            "treasures"       "favorite"        "clean"          
##  [37] "rich"            "fearless"        "fortunate"       "likes"          
##  [41] "earnest"         "generous"        "chances"         "smiles"         
##  [45] "hug"             "kiss"            "approved"        "fond"           
##  [49] "honored"         "consent"         "peace"           "powerful"       
##  [53] "worthy"          "humor"           "entertaining"    "save"           
##  [57] "sincerely"       "festive"         "careful"         "stronger"       
##  [61] "bold"            "eager"           "favored"         "warmth"         
##  [65] "pardon"          "pardons"         "healthy"         "loving"         
##  [69] "chance"          "thoughtful"      "enjoy"           "privileged"     
##  [73] "positively"      "stout"           "encouragement"   "stable"         
##  [77] "smarter"         "ease"            "ambitious"       "improvement"    
##  [81] "hopeful"         "hopes"           "relieved"        "helping"        
##  [85] "cares"           "importance"      "favor"           "tender"         
##  [89] "welcomed"        "treasure"        "spirited"        "secured"        
##  [93] "courtesy"        "calm"            "resolved"        "courageous"     
##  [97] "comfortable"     "sympathy"        "reassuring"      "resolute"       
## [101] "brisk"           "appeased"        "enjoying"        "hoping"         
## [105] "intricate"       "rescued"         "glorious"        "adventures"     
## [109] "friendly"        "astonished"      "reward"          "trusted"        
## [113] "honest"          "clever"          "dear"            "favors"         
## [117] "determined"      "strengthen"      "approval"        "slicker"        
## [121] "sincere"         "jokes"           "joke"            "smartest"       
## [125] "favorites"       "hero"            "adventure"       "abilities"      
## [129] "strongest"       "courteous"       "exasperated"     "enjoys"         
## [133] "rewarded"        "cherished"       "comforting"      "robust"         
## [137] "cherish"         "sympathetic"     "surviving"       "cheered"        
## [141] "worth"           "boldly"          "acquitted"       "unstoppable"    
## [145] "cheer"           "fervent"         "applause"        "cheers"         
## [149] "proudly"         "compassionate"   "bless"           "success"        
## [153] "supported"       "kinder"          "improved"        "defender"       
## [157] "tranquil"        "helpful"         "hail"            "tops"           
## [161] "thankful"        "calmed"          "sunshine"        "opportunity"    
## [165] "inspiration"     "survived"        "gain"            "freedom"        
## [169] "growth"          "futile"          "swiftly"         "satisfied"      
## [173] "congratulations" "confident"       "pardoned"        "energetic"      
## [177] "esteemed"        "benefit"         "secure"          "accomplished"   
## [181] "support"         "rewarding"       "ability"         "jovial"         
## [185] "cheering"        "hailed"          "playful"         "confidence"     
## [189] "consents"        "bargain"         "encouraged"      "relieving"      
## [193] "accomplish"      "resolve"         "cleaner"         "prominent"      
## [197] "serene"          "defenders"       "strengthened"    "wealthy"        
## [201] "revive"
got_afinn2_n <- got_afinn2 %>% 
  count(word, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  mutate(word = fct_reorder(factor(word), n))


ggplot(data = got_afinn2_n, aes(x = word, y = n)) +
  geom_col() +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_bw()

### Overall AFINN summary To summarise the overall sentiment tendency in the text, I calculated the mean and median AFINN scores of the matched words.

The mean and median indicate slightly positive overall sentiments based on the AFINN lexicon.

got_summary <- got_afinn %>% 
  summarize(
    mean_score = mean(value),
    median_score = median(value)
  )
got_summary
## # A tibble: 1 × 2
##   mean_score median_score
##        <dbl>        <dbl>
## 1     -0.542           -1

NRC sentiment analysis

The NRC lexicon was used to group words in Game of Thrones into emotion categories such as fear, trust, joy, sadness, and positive/negative sentiment.

got_nrc <- got_stop %>% 
  inner_join(get_sentiments("nrc"))

The excluded words were counted to identify which frequent terms were missing from the NRC lexicon.

got_exclude <- got_stop %>%
  anti_join(get_sentiments("nrc"))

got_exclude_n <- got_exclude %>%
  count(word, sort = TRUE)

head(got_exclude_n)
## # A tibble: 6 × 2
##   word       n
##   <chr>  <int>
## 1 ser     1023
## 2 jon      787
## 3 ned      743
## 4 tyrion   591
## 5 eyes     567
## 6 hand     567

Now find some counts:

got_nrc_n <- got_nrc %>% 
  count(sentiment, sort = TRUE)


ggplot(data = got_nrc_n, aes(x = sentiment, y = n)) +
  geom_col(aes(fill = sentiment))+
  theme_bw()

The most frequent words within each NRC sentiment category were plotted to show which terms drive the emotional categories.

got_nrc_n5 <- got_nrc %>% 
  count(word,sentiment, sort = TRUE) %>% 
  group_by(sentiment) %>% 
  top_n(5) %>% 
  ungroup()

got_nrc_gg <- ggplot(data = got_nrc_n5, aes(x = reorder(word,n), y = n, fill = sentiment)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~sentiment, ncol = 2, scales = "free") +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(x = "Word", y = "count")

got_nrc_gg

ggsave(plot = got_nrc_gg, 
       here("figures","week13_got_nrc_sentiment.png"), 
       height = 8, 
       width = 5)

Some words in the NRC categories are context-dependent, which shows that lexicon-based sentiment analysis should be interpreted critically.
